_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows the curriculum in Runestone Foundations of Python Programming, Think Python, and OpenStax Introduction to Python Programming. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the sources guide topic selection only.
title: Comprehensions
summary: Build a collection from a short, readable rule.
refresher: A comprehension builds a new collection by looping over an iterable. Write the result expression first, then the `for` part, and add an `if` part only when values should be filtered. Use a normal loop when the rule needs several steps.
refresher_code:
squares = [number * number for number in range(4)]
print(squares)
# [0, 1, 4, 9]

blocks.body: Many loops follow the same pattern: start an empty list, change each value, and append the result. A list comprehension writes that simple pattern as one expression.

blocks.caption: Read the comprehension as: make `number * number` for each number in the range. It creates a new list and does not change the range.
blocks.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4]
squares = [number * number for number in numbers]
print(squares)

blocks.body: An optional `if` filters the input. It decides which items reach the result expression. This is different from an if-else expression, which chooses between two result values.

blocks.caption: The comprehension is useful because the job has one change and one filter. More steps would be easier to follow as a regular loop.
blocks.wrong:
result = [clean(x) if valid(x) else fix(x)
          for x in data if keep(x)]
blocks.right:
even_squares = [n * n for n in numbers
                if n % 2 == 0]

blocks.title: Short does not always mean clear
blocks.body: A comprehension is a good fit when you can say its rule in one breath. Choose a normal loop when you need several conditions, temporary names, logging, or error handling.

blocks.why: The expression `n + 1` runs once for each input value. Adding one to 2, 4, and 6 produces 3, 5, and 7.

blocks.body: Set and dictionary comprehensions use the same reading order. Curly braces with one expression create a set. Curly braces with a key and value separated by a colon create a dictionary.

blocks.prompt: Which expression creates a dictionary that maps each word to its length?
blocks.why: A dictionary comprehension needs a key expression, a colon, and a value expression before the for clause. Here each word is the key and its length is the value.

blocks.title: Comprehension checklist
items: Use a comprehension to create a new collection.
Keep the expression and filter simple.
Do not use one only to cause side effects.
Choose brackets for a list and braces for a set or dictionary.
Switch to a normal loop when the rule becomes hard to read.
